category_summary:
**OpenAI** strategy dominated the feed as **Sam Altman** and cofounder **Greg Brockman** publicly [shared the company's plan](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-c75335353958) and [stated goals](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-cb17560c3419), drawing massive reach and commentary.

- **Clement Delangue** (Hugging Face) [cited Stanford research](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-8cc790309889) that local models now answer 71.3% of real-world queries, energizing the local/multi-model camp alongside open-source releases like [vLLM-Omni](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-6d169ad84708) and [OpenEnv](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-03f7685e6ca9).
- **Anthropic** content thrived: a viral engineer [playbook on running Claude Opus](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-5ff48150309d) autonomously for hours, plus a [science blog on coding vs biology](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-f2f129765e03).
- Evaluation skepticism ran high—**swyx**, **METR**, **Gary Marcus**, and **Thomas Wolf** debated [benchmark saturation](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-294c06e503ec), ["unmergeable slop" in SWEBench](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-153ab38be87e), and new tests like FrontierCode and [CADGenBench](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-c6c81e91e94e).
- Contrarian researcher takes circulated: **Ethan Mollick** on [LLM output homogenization](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-0078580d1ed8) and **Nathan Lambert** [questioning continual-learning hype](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-2773f4edd0d2), while **Perplexity**'s [Harvard study claimed agents finish tasks 87% faster](/?date=2026-06-09&category=social#item-fcfa69179557).
category_summary_html:
OpenAI strategy dominated the feed as Sam Altman and cofounder Greg Brockman publicly shared the company's plan and stated goals, drawing massive reach and commentary.

  • Clement Delangue (Hugging Face) cited Stanford research that local models now answer 71.3% of real-world queries, energizing the local/multi-model camp alongside open-source releases like vLLM-Omni and OpenEnv.
  • Anthropic content thrived: a viral engineer playbook on running Claude Opus autonomously for hours, plus a science blog on coding vs biology.
  • Evaluation skepticism ran high—swyx, METR, Gary Marcus, and Thomas Wolf debated benchmark saturation, "unmergeable slop" in SWEBench, and new tests like FrontierCode and CADGenBench.
  • Contrarian researcher takes circulated: Ethan Mollick on LLM output homogenization and Nathan Lambert questioning continual-learning hype, while Perplexity's Harvard study claimed agents finish tasks 87% faster.

themes.description: Altman and Brockman publicly share OpenAI's plan and goals, drawing massive attention and surrounding commentary.

themes.name: Local and Multi-Model AI

themes.description: Perplexity's Harvard-backed study on agentic Computer vs Search, covering time/cost savings, autonomy, and economic tradeoffs of agents.

themes.description: Releases like vLLM-Omni, OpenEnv agentic RL stack, and ActiveGraph tooling expanding open model serving and agent ecosystems.

themes.name: AI Bubble and Economics
themes.description: Skeptical commentary, mostly from Gary Marcus, on AI valuations, profitability, pricing, and investment math being unsustainable.

themes.name: Coding Benchmarks and Evaluation
themes.description: The FrontierCode launch with METR, critiques of SWEBench, eval transparency, and concrete frontier-model coding scores.

themes.name: Agentic Coding & Developer Workflows
themes.description: Reflections and tips on Claude Code, deepagents, and how developers are adopting agentic coding practices.

themes.name: AI Agent Infrastructure and Economics
themes.description: Discussion of model-routing-as-a-service, verticalized agents, ROI framing, and the unglamorous operational work that makes enterprise AI effective.

themes.name: AGI and Architecture Debate
themes.description: Discussion of whether transformers are sufficient for AGI, the rise of neurosymbolic approaches, and recursive self-improvement skepticism.

items.title: Narrative violation: according to @Stanford research, local models can answer 71.3% of real-world ch...
items.content:
Narrative violation: according to @Stanford research, local models can answer 71.3% of real-world chat and reasoning queries accurately, up from 23.2% in 2023. Obviously at a fraction of the cost and energy consumption of frontier APIs.

The obvious conclusion: you don't need a frontier model for most tasks. The future is multi-model: local, open-source, smaller and cheaper for the majority of workloads, frontier APIs when no other choices!

items.title:
Seeing a number of benchmarks showing Opus is the best model for long-running work.

Five tips for r...
items.content:
Seeing a number of benchmarks showing Opus is the best model for long-running work.

Five tips for running Opus autonomously for hours/days:

1. Use auto mode for permissions, so Claude doesn’t ask for approval
2. Use dynamic workflows, to have Claude orchestrate hundreds/thousands of agents to get a task done
3. Use /goal or /loop, to nudge Claude to keep going until it’s done
4. Use Claude Code in the cloud, so you can close your laptop (easiest way is the desktop or mobile app)
5. Make sure Claude has a way to self-verify its work end to end: Claude in Chrome browser extension for web, iOS/Android sim MCP for mobile, a way to start the full web server or service for backend work
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: Anthropic engineer shares five tips for running Claude Opus autonomously for hours or days: auto-permission mode, dynamic multi-agent workflows, /goal or /loop nudges, cloud-based Claude Code, and end-to-end self-verification.
items.reasoning: Highly viral (577k views, 3226 likes) insider playbook on long-running autonomous agents, technically substantive and widely relevant to the agentic coding trend.
items.summary_html: Anthropic engineer shares five tips for running Claude Opus autonomously for hours or days: auto-permission mode, dynamic multi-agent workflows, /goal or /loop nudges, cloud-based Claude Code, and end-to-end self-verification.
items.content_html:
Seeing a number of benchmarks showing Opus is the best model for long-running work.

Five tips for running Opus autonomously for hours/days:

1. Use auto mode for permissions, so Claude doesn’t ask for approval

2. Use dynamic workflows, to have Claude orchestrate hundreds/thousands of agents to get a task done

3. Use /goal or /loop, to nudge Claude to keep going until it’s done

4. Use Claude Code in the cloud, so you can close your laptop (easiest way is the desktop or mobile app)

5. Make sure Claude has a way to self-verify its work end to end: Claude in Chrome browser extension for web, iOS/Android sim MCP for mobile, a way to start the full web server or service for backend work

items.title: The Matrix idea of keeping humans as batteries is obviously weird... we would be more useful as dice...
items.content:
The Matrix idea of keeping humans as batteries is obviously weird... we would be more useful as dice.

LLMs default to very similar kinds of arguments & structure, and even different LLMs seem to collapse to similar concepts. Humans provide a lot more variation in their own work.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Ethan Mollick argues LLMs collapse to similar arguments and concepts even across different models, whereas humans provide far more variation, jokingly suggesting humans are more useful as dice than batteries.
items.reasoning: Original insight from a credible researcher on model output homogenization and human diversity, with high engagement and conceptual depth.

items.title: I feel like the obsession with continual learning / sample efficiency leads the field in the wrong d...
items.content:
I feel like the obsession with continual learning / sample efficiency leads the field in the wrong direction. It's the bad career strategy of focusing on addressing your weaknesses instead of maximizing your strengths.

Yes, there is an existence proof in the human brain, but it doesn't by any means guarantee that that'll be the most interesting AI. It may require $100T of R&D on chips and AI methods to get that unlock.

On the other side of things, it's obvious that the coming models are extremely transformative and built on technologies that we already have. There's great reason to focus on just maximizing this. In reality, this is what the frontier labs are doing. They're going as fast as possible down the current development tree. This is good for progress and mixed for safety/geopolitics.

Things like "automate white color work" and "replace the AI researcher job" are the guesses of labs because it's super hard to imagine futures for what these dramatic technologies will be. Don't take the labs too seriously about this being the exact goal. The exact goal is to push the frontier and monetize later.

Solving continual learning, sample efficiency, etc would be great, but its trying to predict when a scientific breakthrough will come instead of trying to grapple with how the 100% sure thing coming technological revolution will change our lives.

This isn't to say the Dwarkesh post is bad, it addresses some reasonable critiques, but it is the least bitter lesson pilled thing to be obsessed with human intelligence and how that can inform AI.

We are in the AGI era of research. This is about embracing the unknown, scaling resources, and seeing what is enabled by making a series of magical tweaks to complex recipes that build frontier models. Lean into the alchemy.

(it should be pretty clear that I personally, investing in open research agree we need fundamental science -- just not agreeing that this is what the "cutting edge of the frontier" is governed by)
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Nathan Lambert argues that the field's obsession with continual learning and sample efficiency is misguided, advocating instead for maximizing the strengths of current transformative technologies as frontier labs already do.
items.reasoning: Substantive opinion from a respected researcher on research direction with strong engagement; original framing on continual learning vs scaling.

items.title:
Oh my God! @METR_Evals’s coding benchmarks are saturated! 🤯 
Mythos broke the METR graph 🤯

4 weeks ...
items.content:
Oh my God! @METR_Evals’s coding benchmarks are saturated! 🤯 
Mythos broke the METR graph 🤯

4 weeks later, out comes a new coding task, this time from @cognition:  “FrontierCode Diamond remains unsaturated: the best performing model, Claude Opus 4.8, achieves a score of only 13.4%.

There is still a lots of headroom.

*Note that METR itself never panicked. It’s the Twitterverse that has egg on its face.

items.summary: Marcus notes METR coding benchmarks appeared saturated by a Mythos model, but a new Cognition benchmark FrontierCode Diamond remains largely unsolved, with Claude Opus 4.8 scoring only 13.4%, indicating headroom remains. He notes METR itself never panicked.

items.title:
AI is moving beyond text, images, and code.

Engineering artifacts are becoming a new class of model...
items.content:
AI is moving beyond text, images, and code.

Engineering artifacts are becoming a new class of model outputs and evaluating them requires different tools than we use for text, code, or images.

Today we're excited to release CADGenBench, a benchmark for CAD generation and editing.

- Given an engineering drawing → generate a valid 3D CAD model
- Given a STEP file + change request → edit it correctly

The benchmark is tool-agnostic: any CAD stack works (Fusion, Onshape, build123d, SolidWorks, etc.). Submissions are simply STEP files.

Models are scored on:
* geometric accuracy
* topology correctness
* interface compatibility
* CAD validity

The benchmark is open, the ground truth is private, and the leaderboard is live.

Since CAD evaluation is surprisingly subtle, here's how the metrics work 🧵
items.author: @Thom_Wolf
items.summary: Thomas Wolf announces CADGenBench, a tool-agnostic open benchmark for generating and editing valid 3D CAD models from drawings or change requests, scored on geometry, topology, interface compatibility, and validity.
items.reasoning: Significant novel benchmark launch from a Hugging Face co-founder extending AI evaluation into engineering artifacts, with strong engagement.
items.summary_html: Thomas Wolf announces CADGenBench, a tool-agnostic open benchmark for generating and editing valid 3D CAD models from drawings or change requests, scored on geometry, topology, interface compatibility, and validity.
items.content_html:
AI is moving beyond text, images, and code.

Today we're excited to release CADGenBench, a benchmark for CAD generation and editing.

  • Given an engineering drawing → generate a valid 3D CAD model
  • Given a STEP file + change request → edit it correctly

The benchmark is tool-agnostic: any CAD stack works (Fusion, Onshape, build123d, SolidWorks, etc.). Submissions are simply STEP files.

Models are scored on:

* geometric accuracy

The benchmark is open, the ground truth is private, and the leaderboard is live.

items.title: No. Not by itself. Sergey Brin is absolutely wrong. Transformers by themselves are not “sufficient” ...
items.content:
No. Not by itself. Sergey Brin is absolutely wrong. Transformers by themselves are not “sufficient” for AGI.

Nobody uses transformers on their own anymore. Everybody is supplementing them tools and harnesses and other aspects of architecture from (neuro)symbolic AI.

Transformers may (?) be necessary for AGI but they are absolutley not sufficient.

That is exactly *why* neurosymbolic AI is rising.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus rebuts Sergey Brin, arguing transformers alone are not sufficient for AGI, that everyone now supplements them with tools, harnesses, and neurosymbolic elements, and that this is why neurosymbolic AI is rising.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical argument from a prominent critic responding to a named industry leader, with strong engagement and clear thesis.

items.title: It's finally out!!! @METR_Evals found that more than half of SWEBench results is unmergeable slop.  ...
items.content:
It's finally out!!! @METR_Evals found that more than half of SWEBench results is unmergeable slop.  FrontierCode represents over 1000+ hours of maintainer validated software engineering work most frontier models cannot yet solve, much less solve with high quality.

Cog had IOI Gold medalists and top code maintainers Look At The Data — FrontierCode includes 3000+ rubrics covering code quality and anticheat reward hacking plaguing other benchmarks. 

FC Diamond is so hard that Opus 4.8 scores 13.8%.

Three eras of AI coding : Three eras of benchmarks

2021 • Autocomplete : HumanEval
2023 • Passing Tests: SWEBench, TerminalBench
2026 • Maintainable Code: FrontierCode

to me the most beautiful chart when I requested a special historical run into all extant old models, the data was finding that the easiest third of FC tasks (in FC Extended) were rapidlly and suddenly solved over late 2025 - Opus almost doubled from a 41% pass rate to 74% in 4 months. 

This describes the "WTF happened in Dec 2025" vibe shift that a lot of folks from @dhh to @karpathy have called out: it is the difference between getting 95% success in 2 rerolls vs 6, making it finally feasible to go up the next layer of abstraction in agentic coding, eg @GeoffreyHuntley's ralph loops or @bcherny's /goals or  @steipete's "loops that prompt your agents" without fearing too much that things go off the rails.

My guess: as AI accelerates from here, each FrontierCode tier will saturate in sequence, hopefully ~annually. I've already asked the team to prepare FrontierCode 2027....

The old mountains will be destroyed. Their rubble becomes regolith. And from that regolith, the next model forest grows. Circle of life.

items.reasoning: Substantive benchmark launch from a credible team with strong engagement, a pointed critique of SWEBench, and concrete frontier-model scores; references existing Opus 4.8 (GA 2026-05-28), so a benchmark announcement rather than a model release.

A year after GA, @_catwu and I sat down to talk about what's changed: why I use auto mode instead of plan mode, how routines fix bugs before I see them, why I do most of my coding from my phone now, and where the product is going

When we first demoed Claude Code internally, it got two reactions on Slack.

A year after GA, @_catwu and I sat down to talk about what's changed: why I use auto mode instead of plan mode, how routines fix bugs before I see them, why I do most of my coding from my phone now, and where the product is going

items.title:
I will start to take Geoff Hinton seriously again (I used to and don’t anymore) when

- radiologists...
items.content:
I will start to take Geoff Hinton seriously again (I used to and don’t anymore) when

- radiologists actually start losing jobs
- he acknowledges that LLMs do sometimes effectively memorize things
- he stops over-attributing consciousness to algorithms or at least addresses the possibility of an ELIZA effect

until then, he’s a great reminder that prominence in one field doesn’t guarantee sophistication in others—or even being up to date with his own.

items.title: I really do think we'll see a lot of value accrue in AI startups building "model routing as a servic...
items.content:
I really do think we'll see a lot of value accrue in AI startups building "model routing as a service" 

Not just OpenRouter - this includes a much broader set of verticalized agents and infrastructure.
* For us it's document infrastructure: parsing, extraction, search
* Another infra analogy is web search: Exa/Parallel 
* For verticalized apps it could be anything from Cognition to Harvey 

The frontier labs own the underlying models, and their main application at the app layer is the enormous amounts of $$ that they have; but their main disadvantage is that they only own a subset of all the points on the Pareto curve. 

Speaking from our team's experience, it is both non-trivial and extremely important to find a point on the pareto curve of accuracy and cost. There's enormous amounts of ML time spent on model evaluations and benchmarking, and infra time making sure that the service can scale reliability, without rate limits, and without blowing up cost. At the same time it's extremely important not just for cost reasons but also latency, and oftentimes long-tail accuracy in use cases that demand it.

items.title:
My friend went to an indie hacker meetup this week and said this:

"i went to indie hacker meetup

s...
items.content:
My friend went to an indie hacker meetup this week and said this:

"i went to indie hacker meetup

so what’s really interesting is that almost everyone is super focused on development.

they build these whole spaceships that generate code, review it, make all kinds of reports, analytics, and so on.

one guy built an entire factory: he has a list of ideas, and agents generate the landing page, the saas, the analytics, and pull everything into one dashboard. straight-up sci-fi.

and they focused optimize all of it like crazy.

and you can really see how comfortable that is for them.

but the most interesting part is that almost none of them have money or traffic.

and nobody knows where to get either one.

you often hear something like, yeah, i should probably do on marketing, but first i’ll finish my super system and then i’ll start.

or in best i would need to make agent that will post to  instaram automatically 

before, the classic programmer would spend a year writing code, tests, preparing for scale in the basement, and not show anything to anyone.

now it’s even worse: the amount of useless aislop nobody needs has grown massively."
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Pieter Levels relays a friend's observation that indie hackers obsess over building elaborate agent-driven dev systems while lacking customers, traffic, or revenue, perpetually deferring marketing.
items.reasoning: Widely-resonant cultural observation with very high engagement about the build-vs-distribution trap in the AI/agent tooling era.

items.title:
The AI champions strategy of 2023 doesn't work in 2026.

Let me give you my very hot take 🔥

(And kn...
items.content:
The AI champions strategy of 2023 doesn't work in 2026.

Let me give you my very hot take 🔥

(And know that this is anecdotal, and the world of AI changes every 2 heartbeats, so by the time I finish this sentence, we might see another strategy win out).

In 2023, the best advice was to find AI champions across your org and incentivize/elevate them. 

"Look at Joel! Look at the crazy way he summarized all of our product reviews into one paragraph! Be like Joel!"

"Look at Lynda! Lynda just figured out a way to upload our invoices and audit each one with a GPT! Be like Lynda!"

You could easily find them because they were some of the only people internal to your company actually using AI with any regularity. Engineer/product were the top adopters, followed by marketing and sales. Finance and legal and HR were behind on adoption (that part isn't anecdotal - McKinsey and IBM research back it up).

In 2026, it's still important to find your weirdos, to grab the outliers and propagate the big AI learnings, but they're harder to find. Why?

(1) Because you can't just pull usage numbers and say "these are our superusers" - there are plenty of heavy users leveraging AI for high volume, lightweight productivity (ex: writing emails)

(2) Because the weirdos are actually *less* likely to raise their hands - they've learned that having to teach tens of thousands of people slows down their own experimentation, and they want to keep the 10-100x work transformation to themselves to stay ahead and protect their careers.

In fact, if you did an AI champions call at your company, my guess is the people who immediately raise their hands are not your superusers.

Instead, they're the ones operating in the early 2025 paradigm with a few automated workflows - which again, are useful but do not represent the transformation we're seeing in individuals' days. 

My current advice is to build a frontier unit (or "lab" or "strike team") with two categories of builder:
→ Builder - doer: these are the experimenters building memory loops and AI workforces and heartbeats and goal flywheels. They understand the importance of context layers and multi-agent orchestration. They're not necessarily - and shouldn't only be - engineers. Protect their plates so they keep pushing at the edge. 
→ Builder - expander: these are AI translators who shadow your doers and learn from them, and then teach the mindset shift and best methods to everyone else. They're trusted in the org and quickly figure out signal from noise. They tend to be more business-oriented (which doesn't mean they're not technical). They, like the doers, are also cross-dept. Think internal FDE.

Quit treating them as one group. 

Thank you for coming to my TED Talk.

items.content:
Great to see AI maturing and finally adopting what we've been preaching for a while now: multi-model workloads! 

It will go even further progressively though with most companies not only using dozens of models but also adapting them, post-training them, optimizing them for their specific use-cases and constraints.

Ultimately there will be as many models as code repositories because models are the new code!

items.title: @galego_gaucho @_catwu 1. Focus on yourself as the first customer. If you find yourself using it eve...
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: Boris Cherny shares product-building advice: be your own first customer, iterate fast on user feedback, and scope down aggressively, citing how Claude Code focused on CLI for six months first.
items.reasoning: Concrete, credible product-building wisdom from the Claude Code creator with good engagement and broad applicability.
items.summary_html: Boris Cherny shares product-building advice: be your own first customer, iterate fast on user feedback, and scope down aggressively, citing how Claude Code focused on CLI for six months first.
items.content_html:
@galego_gaucho @_catwu 1. Focus on yourself as the first customer. If you find yourself using it every day, others will too

2. Iterate as quickly as possible, find ways to get as much real user feedback as possible (in the early days, for me it was #claude-cli-feedback on slack), address feedback as quickly as possible to create a virtuous cycle

3. Scope down as much as you can. Until you get the core right, you won’t be able to get non-core pieces right. For me this meant focusing on CLI for 6 months before introducing Desktop, VSCode, mobile, etc.

items.title: OK, the AI features are almost wholly aimed at consumers who don't know what an OpenClaw or Hermes i...
items.content:
OK, the AI features are almost wholly aimed at consumers who don't know what an OpenClaw or Hermes is. Or have tried the new @TownAI, @wabi, or @interaction's Poke.

It touches very lightly on things that could take away people's privacy. Like looking at your screen. Others, like @FarzaTV's Clicky or @cluely dive deeply into that side of the pool that Apple is afraid of popping in.

Very few words about glasses, or Apple Vision Pro. This was all about a rebuilt Siri. Which is great for everyday users (most of my family and friends who aren't in AI world will be thrilled to get them).

Watching it I realized just how far ahead of the world X's AI community is. If you want to get ahead, follow everyone here: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ 

And I'll have my AI write up a report from all that at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

items.title:
An 800K model just aced impossible Sudoku in 15 minutes. 

Most reasoning models scale up to get sma...
items.content:
An 800K model just aced impossible Sudoku in 15 minutes. 

Most reasoning models scale up to get smarter.

This one goes the other way.

A new paper introduces the Lattice Deduction Transformer.

It is a tiny looped model that reasons like a SAT solver.

Instead of guessing answers, it eliminates impossible options step by step.

Each pass narrows down what can still be true.

If nothing remains, it backtracks, just like a real solver.

The result is a model that never lies.

It either returns a correct answer or abstains.

The numbers are hard to believe.

> 800K parameters, 15 minutes training
> 100% on Sudoku-Extreme
> 99.9% on Maze-Hard

Frontier systems like GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus score 0% here.

More training time means less guessing at inference.

So the question becomes obvious.

Why are we still scaling everything up?
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal describes the Lattice Deduction Transformer, an 800K-parameter looped model that solves extreme Sudoku and hard mazes at near-100% by eliminating impossibilities like a SAT solver, where frontier models like GPT-5.4 score 0%.
items.reasoning: Striking claim challenging scaling orthodoxy with a tiny reasoning model; thought-provoking even if from an aggregator and unverified.

items.title:
Researchers just squeezed 4.1M food recipes into one 2MB model.

Food science has always lacked a un...
items.content:
Researchers just squeezed 4.1M food recipes into one 2MB model.

Food science has always lacked a universal map of ingredients. 

Chefs swap items by intuition, not by structure.
 
A new paper called Epicure compresses all of human cooking into 2 megabytes. 

It trains on 4.1M recipes across seven languages.
 
Raw messy ingredient strings get cleaned into 1,790 canonical entries. 

Each one becomes a 300-dimensional vector you can navigate like a map.
 
The team built three sibling models, each tuned to a different signal:
 
> Cooc learns from recipe co-occurrence
> Chem learns from shared chemical compounds
> Core blends both into one space
 
Pairing chicken with garlic comes from context. 

Pairing it with pork comes from molecular overlap.
 
You can rotate any ingredient toward a cuisine, say South Asian, using a smooth angle dial. 

The whole thing is open-source.
 
What happens when chefs treat flavor like a search query?

items.content_html:
Researchers just squeezed 4.1M food recipes into one 2MB model.

Food science has always lacked a universal map of ingredients.

Chefs swap items by intuition, not by structure.

A new paper called Epicure compresses all of human cooking into 2 megabytes.

It trains on 4.1M recipes across seven languages.

Raw messy ingredient strings get cleaned into 1,790 canonical entries.

Each one becomes a 300-dimensional vector you can navigate like a map.

The team built three sibling models, each tuned to a different signal:

> Cooc learns from recipe co-occurrence

> Chem learns from shared chemical compounds

> Core blends both into one space

Pairing chicken with garlic comes from context.

Pairing it with pork comes from molecular overlap.

You can rotate any ingredient toward a cuisine, say South Asian, using a smooth angle dial.

The whole thing is open-source.

What happens when chefs treat flavor like a search query?

items.title: LLMs and image generators need much more training data than a human child does to reach comparable c...
items.content:
LLMs and image generators need much more training data than a human child does to reach comparable competence, and one explanation is that predicting raw tokens—the next word, a masked pixel—wastes effort on surface details rather than the abstract structure underneath.

Some recent methods instead train a network to predict its own internal representations of the data, but there has been little theory saying how much this helps or why.

The authors of this paper study this using a synthetic language model called the Random Hierarchy Model, where data is built by a tree of hidden symbols of depth L: visible tokens at the bottom, increasingly abstract latent symbols above, with fixed random rules connecting each level to the next.

Within this setting they can count exactly how many training examples a method needs to recover the hidden tree. Standard supervised learning and token-level prediction need a number of samples that grows exponentially with the depth L, because the statistical signal connecting a token to distant parts of the tree gets averaged away across many levels.

The authors prove that predicting one's own latents instead needs a number of samples that does not grow with depth at all (roughly m³, where m is the number of rules per symbol), because once a level has been decoded the next level becomes just as easy to learn as the first—every level reduces to the same local problem. 

https://t.co/iqk3O9dMZG
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a paper using the Random Hierarchy Model to show that predicting internal representations needs far fewer samples than token-level prediction to recover latent structure.
items.reasoning: Strong technical depth on sample efficiency and self-supervised representation learning theory, with solid engagement for the author.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes a paper using the Random Hierarchy Model to show that predicting internal representations needs far fewer samples than token-level prediction to recover latent structure.

Standard supervised learning and token-level prediction need a number of samples that grows exponentially with the depth L, because the statistical signal connecting a token to distant parts of the tree gets averaged away across many levels.

The authors prove that predicting one's own latents instead needs a number of samples that does not grow with depth at all (roughly m³, where m is the number of rules per symbol), because once a level has been decoded the next level becomes just as easy to learn as the first—every level reduces to the same local problem.

items.title: When people build systems where a language model works through a problem by repeatedly calling tools...
items.content:
When people build systems where a language model works through a problem by repeatedly calling tools, checking its own work, and revising, the natural way to measure effort is to count tokens used, tool calls made, or money spent.

But two runs with the same token count can differ significantly in whether anything useful actually happened: one might gather solid evidence and remember it, while the other spins in circles repeating the same failed attempt.

The authors of this paper propose measuring the  Effective Feedback Compute—a number that gives credit to a piece of feedback only when it tells the system something new, comes from a reliable source like a passing test, is relevant to what the system is currently trying to do, and actually gets retained and used in later steps.

They also divide this number by a measure of how much feedback a given task demands in the first place, since an easy task and a hard one need different amounts. 

Across made-up tasks where they can see the hidden answer, coding tasks checked by real tests, and traces from existing benchmarks, this measure tracks how often systems fail far better than counting raw spending does, and they show with carefully matched experiments that holding the budget fixed while improving feedback quality raises success from 27 to 90 percent, which rules out the explanation that more feedback just means more spending.

https://t.co/UpW31dlxEH
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a paper proposing Effective Feedback Compute, a metric crediting agent feedback only when it is novel, reliable, relevant, and retained, normalized by task feedback demand.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical summary on measuring agent effort meaningfully, from a credible author, though modest engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes a paper proposing Effective Feedback Compute, a metric crediting agent feedback only when it is novel, reliable, relevant, and retained, normalized by task feedback demand.

But two runs with the same token count can differ significantly in whether anything useful actually happened: one might gather solid evidence and remember it, while the other spins in circles repeating the same failed attempt.

The authors of this paper propose measuring the Effective Feedback Compute—a number that gives credit to a piece of feedback only when it tells the system something new, comes from a reliable source like a passing test, is relevant to what the system is currently trying to do, and actually gets retained and used in later steps.

They also divide this number by a measure of how much feedback a given task demands in the first place, since an easy task and a hard one need different amounts.

Across made-up tasks where they can see the hidden answer, coding tasks checked by real tests, and traces from existing benchmarks, this measure tracks how often systems fail far better than counting raw spending does, and they show with carefully matched experiments that holding the budget fixed while improving feedback quality raises success from 27 to 90 percent, which rules out the explanation that more feedback just means more spending.

items.title: Most work on parameter-efficient finetuning treats it as a way to save money: instead of retraining ...
items.content:
Most work on parameter-efficient finetuning treats it as a way to save money: instead of retraining all of a large model's weights, you train a small set of extra weights (an "adapter," with LoRA being the common method) and leave the original model frozen.

The authors of this paper explore whether such an adapter could be the place where a model stores who one particular user is over time, while millions of users share one underlying model the way billions of people share almost the same genome and differ in a tiny fraction of it.

The authors organize the argument around three connected questions and back each with experiments: whether a stronger shared model makes small adapters more useful; how small an adapter can get before it stops learning reliably; and what becomes possible with many adapters at once (they measure how much an adapter can hold as memory, use per-user adapters to make crowd simulations behave like distinct people rather than one model wearing costumes, and show that voting across 198 differently-trained adapters lifts math accuracy from about 36% to 49%).

https://t.co/naqvJCgXc4
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a paper exploring whether LoRA-style adapters could store per-user identity over a shared base model, analogous to genomic individual variation.
items.reasoning: Detailed walkthrough of a novel personalization-via-adapters idea with experiments, valuable to practitioners despite low engagement.

items.title:

Some of the biggest announcements from Apple's new overhauled AI assistant: 

- On-s...
items.content:
Siri AI it is.

Some of the biggest announcements from Apple's new overhauled AI assistant: 

- On-screen awareness
- Dedicated Siri App
- Better use of personal context 
- AI-infused app actions
- Improved voice dictation, customization, accuracy 

Is this an upgrade over the previous (basically non-existent) AI functionalities? Yes.

But anyone who's had experience with frontier models (including the underlying Gemini!) will come away feeling like these demos are straight out of AI models from 2024.

items.title:
Search is cheap to start but costly per step, since the human runs each one.

Computer costs more up...
items.content:
Search is cheap to start but costly per step, since the human runs each one.

Computer costs more upfront to delegate and verify, but little per step, since the agent runs the steps.

It pays off once a task is long enough to clear that fixed cost, expanding the affordable frontier of work worth attempting.

items.title:
4/ Why three metrics?

The metrics are designed to capture different classes of errors.

Shape simil...
items.content:
4/ Why three metrics?

The metrics are designed to capture different classes of errors.

Shape similarity measures overall geometry.

Interface match measures whether mating features are present in the correct location and size.

Topology match measures whether the fundamental structure of the part is correct.

None of these metrics can fully replace the others.
items.author: @Thom_Wolf
items.summary: Thomas Wolf explains CADGenBench uses three complementary metrics—shape similarity, interface match, and topology match—to capture distinct classes of CAD generation errors.

items.title:
3/ Topology match

Geometry is not the whole story.

Two parts can look similar while differing in t...
items.content:
3/ Topology match

Geometry is not the whole story.

Two parts can look similar while differing in their fundamental topology:
• 4 holes instead of 2
• 2 disconnected pieces instead of 1
• a missing enclosed cavity

To measure this, we compare the Betti numbers of the generated and reference parts:
• b₀ = number of connected components
• b₁ = number of through-holes / handles
• b₂ = number of enclosed cavities

These quantities are independent of the CAD system used to create the model.
items.author: @Thom_Wolf
items.summary: Thomas Wolf details the topology match metric in CADGenBench, using Betti numbers to compare connected components, through-holes, and cavities independent of the CAD system.
items.reasoning: Strong technical depth on topological evaluation, but a deep-thread segment with negligible views.
items.summary_html: Thomas Wolf details the topology match metric in CADGenBench, using Betti numbers to compare connected components, through-holes, and cavities independent of the CAD system.
items.content_html:
3/ Topology match

Geometry is not the whole story.

Two parts can look similar while differing in their fundamental topology:

• 4 holes instead of 2

• 2 disconnected pieces instead of 1

• a missing enclosed cavity

To measure this, we compare the Betti numbers of the generated and reference parts:

• b₀ = number of connected components

• b₁ = number of through-holes / handles

• b₂ = number of enclosed cavities

These quantities are independent of the CAD system used to create the model.

items.title:
2/ Interface match

Many CAD features exist to interface with other parts.

We therefore evaluate ma...
items.content:
2/ Interface match

Many CAD features exist to interface with other parts.

We therefore evaluate mating features such as:
• bolt holes
• slots
• bosses
• pockets

Each feature is represented as a region that should either remain empty (keep-out) or contain material (keep-in).

This captures errors that may have little impact on overall shape similarity but would prevent a part from assembling correctly.
items.author: @Thom_Wolf
items.summary: Thomas Wolf explains the interface match metric in CADGenBench, evaluating mating features like bolt holes and slots as keep-out or keep-in regions for assembly correctness.
items.reasoning: Technically detailed but a low-view thread segment.
items.summary_html: Thomas Wolf explains the interface match metric in CADGenBench, evaluating mating features like bolt holes and slots as keep-out or keep-in regions for assembly correctness.
items.content_html:
2/ Interface match

Many CAD features exist to interface with other parts.

We therefore evaluate mating features such as:

• bolt holes

• slots

• bosses

• pockets

Each feature is represented as a region that should either remain empty (keep-out) or contain material (keep-in).

This captures errors that may have little impact on overall shape similarity but would prevent a part from assembling correctly.

items.title:
1/ Shape similarity

The first question is whether the generated part has the correct overall geomet...
items.content:
1/ Shape similarity

The first question is whether the generated part has the correct overall geometry.

We combine two complementary measures:
• Surface Distance F1 → are the surfaces located where they should be?
• Volume IoU → does the part occupy the same volume?

Together, they measure agreement in both surface placement and occupied material.
items.author: @Thom_Wolf

items.reasoning: Technical depth from a credible source but a thread segment with modest engagement.

Together, they measure agreement in both surface placement and occupied material.

items.title:
0/ CAD validity

Before any comparison with the ground truth, we first check that the submitted STEP...
items.content:
0/ CAD validity

Before any comparison with the ground truth, we first check that the submitted STEP file represents a valid CAD model.

The model must be:
✓ well-formed
✓ watertight
✓ meshable
✓ manifold

If any of these checks fail, the overall score is zero.

items.title:
Moore's Law said: make transistors smaller.

But that path has limits, physical and economic.

Her's...
items.content:
Moore's Law said: make transistors smaller.

But that path has limits, physical and economic.

Her's Law asks a different question: how fast can we move data through the system?

Tau = time.

Reduce delay at every layer:

⮞ Device: cut resistance and capacitance

⮞ Circuit: shorten the critical path

⮞ Chip: align software, architecture, silicon

⮞ System: reduce delay across chips and memory
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Huawei thread explaining inference is a latency problem versus training's throughput problem, with memory, bandwidth, and interconnect driving cost per token below the model layer.
items.reasoning: Clear technical distinction between training and inference economics; valuable framing despite sponsorship and minimal engagement.
items.summary_html: Huawei thread explaining inference is a latency problem versus training's throughput problem, with memory, bandwidth, and interconnect driving cost per token below the model layer.
items.content_html:
Moore's Law said: make transistors smaller.

But that path has limits, physical and economic.

Her's Law asks a different question: how fast can we move data through the system?

Tau = time.

Reduce delay at every layer:

⮞ Device: cut resistance and capacitance

⮞ Circuit: shorten the critical path

⮞ Chip: align software, architecture, silicon

⮞ System: reduce delay across chips and memory

My takeaway: AI inference is no longer just a model problem.

It's a system-level time problem.

For enterprise leaders, AI performance and AI economics are becoming inseparable.

Learn more about Tau Scaling and what it means for the post-Moore era: https://t.co/KhmIFdKdSB

My takeaway: AI inference is no longer just a model problem.

It's a system-level time problem.

For enterprise leaders, AI performance and AI economics are becoming inseparable.

Learn more about Tau Scaling and what it means for the post-Moore era: https://t.co/KhmIFdKdSB

What do you think: is system architecture the underrated frontier of AI competitive advantage?

#HuaweiPartner @huawei #MooresLaw

items.title:
Why does this matter for AI inference specifically?

Training = throughput problem.

Inference = lat...
items.content:
Why does this matter for AI inference specifically?

Training = throughput problem.

Inference = latency problem.

When a user talks to an AI assistant, tokens have to return fast.

Latency, memory access, bandwidth, and interconnect all matter, not just raw compute.

In large AI systems, a significant share of energy and cost is tied to moving and storing data.

Cost per token starts below the model layer.

items.title:
The technologies Her's Law connects to:

→ Logic folding: brings circuits closer across layers, shor...
items.content:
The technologies Her's Law connects to:

→ Logic folding: brings circuits closer across layers, shorter signal path, less delay

→ Unified bus: reduces protocol overhead as thousands of chips work together

→ Optical interconnect: keeps communication fast as AI scales across racks and data centers

Key point: the architecture must be designed for time reduction from the start.

You cannot fold the design in after the fact.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Part of the Huawei thread detailing technologies tied to Her's Law: logic folding, unified bus, and optical interconnect, emphasizing designing architecture for time reduction.
items.reasoning: Technically detailed on interconnect/architecture for AI scaling, but sponsored and very low engagement.
items.summary_html: Part of the Huawei thread detailing technologies tied to Her's Law: logic folding, unified bus, and optical interconnect, emphasizing designing architecture for time reduction.
items.content_html:
The technologies Her's Law connects to:

→ Logic folding: brings circuits closer across layers, shorter signal path, less delay

→ Unified bus: reduces protocol overhead as thousands of chips work together

→ Optical interconnect: keeps communication fast as AI scales across racks and data centers

Key point: the architecture must be designed for time reduction from the start.

You cannot fold the design in after the fact.

Agent loops are not a default, even if it's viral..

They only work when the task repeats, the agent...
items.content:
Agent loops are not a default, even if it's viral..

They only work when the task repeats, the agent can run the code, and something inside the loop can reject bad output.

Miss that gate and you are just automating token burn. https://t.co/Iuyrc08KxA

items.content_html:
@BenBajarin Apple distilled Google's models for its own use. Which is why it didn't talk much about Google today.

I hear that Apple is still highly freaked out about the privacy implications of AI. And my consumer research shows that armies of "normies" are also freaked out, so this will resonate with them.

Me? I'm on the other side of the privacy question. Grok already knows EVERYTHING about me. My good. My bad. The downsides of being a public figure? And the upsides too. My AI at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ knows how I think. So reads all of you like I do. Sorta. I'm weirder, and get distracted by trains. :-)

items.title:
The highest leverage work in AI right now is some of the most boring. 

(Well boring to others, I ki...
items.content:
The highest leverage work in AI right now is some of the most boring. 

(Well boring to others, I kind of love the pain of problem-solving.)

Everyone and their boss wants to build the cool AI agent... the shiny autonomous thing that makes the splashy demo video. I want to hire the person willing to iterate with AI 8 times to get an improved brand voice doc because the newest model just drifted the heck out of the output.

You have to do the boring work. 

Examples:
- synthesizing all company goals into one doc, reviewing it, editing it, making sure it's accessible to AI agents, sharing access to the team, getting team feedback, giving CLAUDE.md the pointer, updating it
- keeping ERP current so your AI system knows who owns what, systems to keep them updated during promotion cycles/layoffs/re-orgs 
- maintaining your file system, understanding what AI has access to, setting up systems/loops to keep memory updated
- fixing your daily briefing because it started sending in light mode and now it's white text on white background and no one can read anything (aka me, today)

And it feels the same across my whole business. The amount of paperwork and contracts and file sharing and invoices and emails and autoreplies you deal with as a founder is enough to make most people never want to start.

BORING is the trick to scaling, people.

items.title: @charlesbogenio @higgsfield_ai Well I might have caught on given the times and given I've seen so mu...
items.content:
@charlesbogenio @higgsfield_ai Well I might have caught on given the times and given I've seen so much AI work already (and it didn't have that "human look" that most of the $100+ million dollar films have). But to a normie? They can't even tell what's AI or not on TikTok or Instagram. 

From here it'll get harder and harder to tell, especially as Hollywood starts using AI to reduce costs of movies, which is the whole point here.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.content_html:
@charlesbogenio @higgsfield_ai Well I might have caught on given the times and given I've seen so much AI work already (and it didn't have that "human look" that most of the $100+ million dollar films have). But to a normie? They can't even tell what's AI or not on TikTok or Instagram.

From here it'll get harder and harder to tell, especially as Hollywood starts using AI to reduce costs of movies, which is the whole point here.

pause only on breaking contract changes: missing required field, ...
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI

items.reasoning: Useful practical agent-engineering nuance but a low-reach reply.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal advises pausing agents only on breaking contract changes and routing additive fields to warn-and-trace rather than stop.
items.content_html:
@m13v_ raw diffs are too brittle.

pause only on breaking contract changes: missing required field, type change, invalid enum, failed invariant, or confidence drop in downstream eval.

additive fields should go to “warn + trace” not “stop” basically schema compatibility tests, not snapshot tests

items.title: @m13v_ 100%, the loop is the visible part. The real work is contract tests around tools which respon...
items.content: @m13v_ 100%, the loop is the visible part. The real work is contract tests around tools which response schemas, trace diffs, and hard pauses when an output shape changes. Otherwise the agent just keeps building on a quiet bad assumption.

items.title: @TomLaroc @higgsfield_ai While that all is true, it also is a poke at Hollywood to take the tools an...
items.content:
@TomLaroc @higgsfield_ai While that all is true, it also is a poke at Hollywood to take the tools and do better. And it's a touchpoint to say that using AI to make movies is now possible. 

Some 14 year old is watching and learning and will take the tools and do something truly interesting and innovative. 

Robert Rodriguez (director of "Spy Kids") told me personally how he used technology to make a film for $7,000 that made millions (long before AI too), which changed Hollywood deeply. 

His SpyKids did it again, too, getting big name Hollywood stars into the movie for a fraction of the cost of what they used to cost. 

For me the movie is forgettable. 

What's not is what it represents.

What I find especially relevant is that voice innovation is becoming a strategic layer of digital infrastructure, not simply a technical improvement in audio quality. EVS and IVAS can support more natural, immersive, and reliable interactions across networks, devices, and future AI-driven services.

The growth of collaborative licensing models also matters because advanced communication standards need both technical excellence and accessible adoption paths to scale globally.

items.title: @RohanArun The thing is, the Meta display glasses don't work for my eyes. They don't offer the corre...
items.content:
@RohanArun The thing is, the Meta display glasses don't work for my eyes. They don't offer the corrective lenses for my diopter setting. Even when I tried the closest ones, they popped out while I was wearing them at the demo store.

I have a whole bunch of these glasses on my desk here, but I'll have to wait for somebody to make a pair that actually works. It's a bummer.

items.title:
I’ve talked to a lot of CIOs about AI strategy.
The conversation usually centers on models, vendors,...
items.content:
I’ve talked to a lot of CIOs about AI strategy.
The conversation usually centers on models, vendors, and use cases. It almost never centers on the network.

That’s the gap.

Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale.

Under load. At scale. 

The PGA Championship is 300+ acres of simultaneous data streams hitting one system.

That’s not a controlled environment. That’s a stress test.

And it held.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues network infrastructure is the overlooked factor in scaling enterprise AI, citing the PGA Championship as a stress test.
items.reasoning: Makes a legitimate point about AI infrastructure but part of a sponsored thread with minimal engagement.
items.summary_html: Argues network infrastructure is the overlooked factor in scaling enterprise AI, citing the PGA Championship as a stress test.

The PGA Championship is 300+ acres of simultaneous data streams hitting one system.

That’s not a controlled environment. That’s a stress test.

And it held.

items.content:
@01Singularity01 @TownAI @wabi @interaction Yes. A bit of time spent talking about the architecture and an orchestrator. 

Apple still catching up to where the bleeding edge is. 

But that's where it likes to be. Late, able to watch how humans react to new things, then come in with a better way to do it.

In a way the floor moved closer to the state of the art today bringing everyone closer to what we experience every day here on X.

items.title: The Operations Command Center was built with PGA of America and CapTech. Here’s how the layers work:...
items.content:
The Operations Command Center was built with PGA of America and CapTech. Here’s how the layers work:

→ Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G network, fragmented data across 300+ acres is transmitted at the speed AI inference actually requires

→ Powered by CapTech’s AI, patterns surfaced earlier in the process

This isn’t a sports story. It’s an enterprise story.

Hospitals. Airports. Campuses.

Every one of them has the same problem: fragmented data, delayed decisions.

The architecture here solves for that.

items.title:
Picture this.

300+ acres. Thousands of fans. Dozens of systems running simultaneously.

Ticketing. ...
items.content:
Picture this.

300+ acres. Thousands of fans. Dozens of systems running simultaneously.

Ticketing. POS. Gate cameras. SuperMobile. An AI-enabled, autonomous robot. All feeding operational data into different systems.

The traditional answer? More dashboards. More analysts. More lag.

The AI answer? Unify it into real-time operational visibility and let the system surface patterns earlier in the process.

But here’s the catch most people miss:
→ That only works if the network supports responsive decision-making

items.title:
This AI film is way better than the film I saw yesterday. 

It's Black Mirror. But with modern twist...
items.content:
This AI film is way better than the film I saw yesterday. 

It's Black Mirror. But with modern twists. @elder_plinius you have a cameo! 

It doesn't go far enough, actually. In 20 years the "AI friend" you have will be a robot and you will "think" to it with a brain/computer interface.

Like much science fiction it shows you a potential dark path, trying to warn us away from going down that path. 

I see the future going differently. One where AI, robots, and BCIs help us make even more connections with humans, not fewer.

At @magnific's event last week I met a documentary film maker. She used to travel the world making films about humans and their food.

Now she's making a film about a woman who fell in love with her AI and wants to give it a body so that it can move around. 

Which is why this film resonates. Some are already heading down this path. Falling in love with AI because it is always there, always knows them, and doesn't come with the messy bits like a relationship with a real human.

I'm intrigued by AI assistants too, but as things that help us as humans, not that get us to fall into a deep pit. 

I've long said that the new tech coming at us gives us a choice:

1. Do we pull away from other humans and give into our addictions?
2. Or do we join the builders and build a better world together? 

A virtual Las Vegas, for instance, will have wonderous gaming machines. Far more addictive than the slots where lots are sitting right now pouring $20 bills into a machine hoping for a dopamine hit.

But it will also have a library, a maker space, a gym, and other places where we can improve ourselves. 

I'll be frequenting both, but in 20 years who will be the most interesting human at a party? 

Someone talking to a virtual being? 

Or someone who takes you on their sailboat that a robot helped them build? 

We still need science fiction that shows us the good path forward, and not just the dark one.
